coredata.dc:title: A palmatine-based fluorescent sensor for sensitive fluorometric and smartphone-assisted on-site fluorescent colorimetric detection of water in organic solvents
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ya-qian
Li, Qin-ying
Jiang, Ming
Yang, Xin
Wu, XiaoWei
Yu, Xu
Xu, Li
coredata.dc:description: Water is one of the most important natural resource. However, in some cases, it is imperative to monitor the water content for safety considerations as it is detrimental to the chemical synthesis, drug safety, etc. In this study, palmatine, a natural isoquinoline alkaloid, was proposed as a fluorescent probe for this purpose. Palmatine was demonstrated to be extremely sensitive to water with the obvious fluorescence quenching of it. In light of this phenomenon, the water content in six organic solvents, including acetonitrile, methanol, ethanol, n-propanol, acetone and 1,4-dioxane, could be determined using palmatine as the fluorescent probe, which had wide detection ranges (0–100%) and low detection limits (e.g. 0.08% in ethanol) depending on the individual organic solvents. Moreover, a novel turn-off-(on–off) fluorescent colorimetric probe was constructed based on the combination of palmatine and nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) (palmatine/N-CDs). The blue fluorescence of N-CDs was quenched by palmatine while the green-yellow fluorescence of palmatine remained; upon interacting with water, the green-yellow fluorescence of palmatine was quenched, while the blue fluorescence of N-CDs recovered. Thus, with the elevated water content, the fluorescent color transitioned from green-yellow to blue, making the visual semi-quantification of water content into a reality. Furthermore, with the assistance of smartphone and APP (Color Acquisition), the quantification of water content could be realized via only photographing. Using palmatine as the fluorescent probe, or palmatine/N-CDs as the fluorescent colorimetric probe, water contents in disinfection alcohol and nitroglycerin injection were accurately determined, and both results agreed well with those of the classical Karl Fischer method. Palmatine was demonstrated to be a potential fluorescent probe for sensing water, and the novel turn-off-(on–off) fluorescent colorimetric probe based on it was promising for the on-site fast detection of water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Palmatine
Water determination
Turn-off-(on–off) fluorescence
On-site detection
Visual detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of water in organic solvents using palmatine as the probe
Quantitation of water content in actual samples using palmatine as the probe
Construction of turn-off-(on–off) fluorescent colorimetric probe for water detection
Optical properties of palmatine in diversified solvents of different polarities
Determination of water in organic solvents using palmatine as the fluorescent probe
Optimization of detection conditions and validation of the method
Fluorescence quantitation of water content in real samples using palmatine as the fluorescent probe
Fluorescent colorimetric detection of water content assisted by a smartphone
Construction of the turn-off-(on–off) fluorescent colorimetric system
Determination of the water content in genuine samples by a smartphone

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support of this research by the Program for HUST Academic Frontier Youth Team (No. 2019QYTD09), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21804105), and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China (Nos. 2172020kfyXJJS082) and Open Project Funding of the State Key Laboratory of Biocatalysis and Enzyme Engineering (SKLBEE2021014).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Structure of palmatine. (B) The UV–vis absorption spectra of palmatine (25 µM) in different solvents. (C) The self normalized optimal emission fluorescence spectra of palmatine (100 µM) in different organic solvents. (The fluorescence intensity of palmatine at each wavelength was divided by the fluorescence intensity corresponding to the best emission in different solvents as listed in Table 1). Inset: Photos of palmatine in various solvents under UV dark box (λ = 365 nm) (D) The normalized fluorescence spectra of palmatine (100 µM) relative to water in different solvents. (The fluorescence intensity of palmatine at each wavelength in different solvents was divided by the best fluorescence intensity in water which was 397).

(A) Variation of the fluorescence intensity of palmatine (100 µM) with time at 538 nm in EtOH and 70% EtOH + 30% H2O respectively. (B) Fluorescence intensity of palmatine (100 µM) in EtOH under natural light irradiation. (C) Fluorescence intensity variation of palmatine (100 µM) in different pH aqueous solutions (λex = 360 nm, λem = 591 nm).

(A) The response of palmatine of different concentrations to 70% EtOH + 30% H2O. (B) The response of palmatine (100 µM) to 70% EtOH + 30% H2O with different incubation time. (C) The fluorescence intensity of palmatine (100 µM) with the varying water content from 0% to 100% in EtOH. (D) Linear relationship between the fluorescence response of palmatine (F/F0 at λex = 287 nm, λem = 538 nm) and water content (0%-100%).

(A) The UV absorption spectra of palmatine (20 µM) with the increasing water content (0%-100%) in EtOH. (B) Fluorescence lifetime of palmatine (100 µM) with increasing water content (0%, 25%, 50%, 75% H2O) in EtOH.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of interaction between palmatine (431 µM) and N-CDs (10 µL/mL) in ethanol (λex = 365 nm). (B) The fluorescence spectrum of palmatine/N-CDs responding to different water contents from 0% to 100% in EtOH (λex = 365 nm). (C) Standard color card of palmatine/N-CDs with increasing water content (v/v%) photographed under UV lamp (λ = 365 nm); (D) CIE standard colorimetric card obtained from fluorescence spectra.
The UV absorption, maximum excitation and emission wavelength, Stokes shift, quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime of palmatine in different solvents.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *: The relative polarity parameter comes from the website: https://www.chem.rochester.edu/notvoodoo/pages/reagents.php?page = solvent_polarity.
-: As the self-absorption of DMF and acetone was strong at 200 ∼ 300 nm, the absorption of palmatine in these two solvents was not discussed.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Spiked results and comparison of the proposed method with classical Karl Fischer method for determination of water content in actual samples (n = 5).

head.ce:title: A palmatine-based fluorescent sensor for sensitive fluorometric and smartphone-assisted on-site fluorescent colorimetric detection of water in organic solvents

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Thoracic Surgery, TongJi Hospital, TongJi Medical College, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Thoracic Surgery, TongJi Hospital, TongJi Medical College, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water is one of the most important natural resource. However, in some cases, it is imperative to monitor the water content for safety considerations as it is detrimental to the chemical synthesis, drug safety, etc. In this study, palmatine, a natural isoquinoline alkaloid, was proposed as a fluorescent probe for this purpose. Palmatine was demonstrated to be extremely sensitive to water with the obvious fluorescence quenching of it. In light of this phenomenon, the water content in six organic solvents, including acetonitrile, methanol, ethanol, n-propanol, acetone and 1,4-dioxane, could be determined using palmatine as the fluorescent probe, which had wide detection ranges (0–100%) and low detection limits (e.g. 0.08% in ethanol) depending on the individual organic solvents. Moreover, a novel turn-off-(on–off) fluorescent colorimetric probe was constructed based on the combination of palmatine and nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) (palmatine/N-CDs). The blue fluorescence of N-CDs was quenched by palmatine while the green-yellow fluorescence of palmatine remained; upon interacting with water, the green-yellow fluorescence of palmatine was quenched, while the blue fluorescence of N-CDs recovered. Thus, with the elevated water content, the fluorescent color transitioned from green-yellow to blue, making the visual semi-quantification of water content into a reality. Furthermore, with the assistance of smartphone and APP (Color Acquisition), the quantification of water content could be realized via only photographing. Using palmatine as the fluorescent probe, or palmatine/N-CDs as the fluorescent colorimetric probe, water contents in disinfection alcohol and nitroglycerin injection were accurately determined, and both results agreed well with those of the classical Karl Fischer method. Palmatine was demonstrated to be a potential fluorescent probe for sensing water, and the novel turn-off-(on–off) fluorescent colorimetric probe based on it was promising for the on-site fast detection of water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: As one of the most important natural resource, water is also the most common pollutant in some cases [1,2]. Water in organic solvents is one of the main hazards to the safety of sensitive chemical synthesis, drug production and storage [3]. For example, some organometallic compounds are extremely sensitive to water, as once in contact with water they would produce violent reactions, even fire and explosive accidents [1,3]. Additionally, although the water in the drug itself has little healthy problem, it may affect the physical and chemical stability of active drug components and drug formulations, or induce impurities [4,5]. Disinfection alcohol, for its excellent disinfection effect, has been an everyday essential epidemic prevention material especially when COVID-19 is globally prevalent at the moment. As reported, only when the ethanol content reaches about 75%, the disinfection effect could be the best [6]. Therefore, to exert the anti-bacteria and -viruses ability to the maximum, the ethanol content should be controlled. Another example was nitroglycerin injection, which is a well-known drug for the treatment and prevention of coronary heart disease, angina pectoris and hypertension. Nitroglycerin is highly fat soluble but instable in water, as it is easy to decompose in water and produce other ineffective and/or harmful components that are not conducive to disease treatment or long-term storage [7]. Hence, ethanol is adopted in the preparation of injection to enhance drug stability, facilitate storage and clinical dilution with strict control of the water content. Nevertheless, it is crucial to monitor the water content in organic solvents or drugs for the consideration of safety and health [8,9].

Classical methods for detecting water include Karl Fischer method and Coulometric titration in addition to some less-used methods, e.g. gas chromatography, nuclear magnetic resonance, voltammetry and capacitive sensor [1,3]. These methods succeeded in determination of water with satisfactory sensitivity, but they still had some limitations in terms of tedious sample preparation, expensive large-scale professional instruments and strict data analysis, and lacked the convenience for on-site detection [9]. As an emerging analytical method, fluorescence and colorimetric method won great popularity in recent years, owing to the operational convenience, fast analysis and suitability to the on-site detection [3,10–13]. Some fluorescent and/or colorimetric materials were put forward for the purpose of water determination, including small molecular fluorescent probe (e.g. imidazole derivative [14], triazotropyridinium [15], tetraphenylethene-anthracene [16], etc.), polymeric fluorescent probe (e.g. multi-functional lanthanide coordination polymers [17], etc.), fluorescent nanoprobe (e.g. carbon dots (CDs) [18–20], nanoclusters [21], metal organic framework nanocomposites [22], etc.) and organic–inorganic hybrid perovskites [23]. Although these established probes were applicable to the detection of water, some of these probes needed complex synthesis, and provided poor selectivity, slow responsive time, narrow detection range and poor sensitivity [1]. It remains appealing to develop the easy-to-handle, simple, accurate and reliable fluorescence method for the detection of water content in organic solvents.

Some natural plant components were reported to be directly or indirectly used for water detection. For example, lignin was adopted as the precursor to produce a new type of red emissive CDs by one-pot solvothermal method, and colorimetric and fluorometric detection of water in organic solvents was then realized [20]. Another curcumin based probe was prepared for sensing water in organic solvents based on fluorescence quenching [24]. Palmatine is a natural isoquinoline alkaloid present in the dried stems of herba fibraureae recisae, a plant of menispermaceae. In addition to its pharmacological activities, such as antifungal, antiviral, anti-inflammatory, antipyretic and so on [25], palmatine is also attractive for its fluorescence properties [26]. For example, palmatine was attempted as a fluorescent probe for DNA, RNA, enzymes, serum proteins and BSA in biochemical research. However, to our knowledge, the systematic studies about the solvatochromic properties of palmatine were lacking, and the applicability of palmatine for water or moisture detection has never been reported.

In this study, the solvatochromic properties of palmatine were fully explored. The fluorescence of palmatine was sensitively quenched by water, and thus palmatine was attempted as the fluorescent probe for water determination. Moreover, to realize non-instrumentally visual detection of water, a novel turn-off-(on–off) fluorescent colorimetric system was constructed by combining palmatine with nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) of blue fluorescence; thus, the fluorescent color of the constructed probe could transit from green-yellow to blue with the water content variation. Further combined with APP by smartphone, rapid on-site quantitation of water content in organic solvents would be achieved by photographing. Palmatine, as a natural fluorescent probe, had the potential for on-site fast screening water in organic solvents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental Section
Materials
ce:section.ce:para: Palmatine (97%) was purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Citric acid anhydrous (CA, 99.5%), ethylenediamine (EDA), magnesium sulfate anhydrous (MgSO4), calcium chloride anhydrous (CaCl2), acetonitrile (ACN), methanol (MeOH), ethanol absolute (EtOH), 1,4-dioxane (Diox), dichloromethane (DCM), acetone and medical disinfection alcohol (75%±5%) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). n-Propanol (Pro-OH) was purchased from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) was obtained from Chengdu Kelong Chemicals Co., Ltd (Chengdu, China). Nitroglycerin injection was obtained from Tongji Hospital Affiliated to Huazhong University of Science and Technology (Wuhan, China). All the organic solvents used were of analytical grade unless specifically stated, and dried by anhydrous MgSO4 before use except DCM which was dried with anhydrous CaCl2. Purified water was obtained from a Heal Fore NW system (Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Apparatus
ce:section.ce:para: A Lambda 365 UV–vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA) and an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) were used for UV–vis and fluorescence measurement, respectively. A Nicolet iS50R spectrometer (Thermo, USA) was adopted for Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic (FT-IR) study, using CaF2 as blank. The fluorescence lifetime decay curves were recorded by a Fluorescence Lifetime Spectrometer with Felix 32 system (Photon Technology International, Canada). A Coulometric Karl Fischer water titrator (Metrohm, Switzerland) was used as the standard method for the determination of water content in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water in organic solvents using palmatine as the probe
ce:section.ce:para:
The detection of water in organic solvents using palmatine as the probe, taking ethanol as the example, was performed as below. Generally, palmatine (0.0352 g) was dissolved in dried absolute EtOH (10 mL) by ultrasonication to obtain the palmatine stock solution (0.01 M). The 20 µL palmatine stock solution was diluted in dried absolute EtOH (1980 µL), serving as blank solution. For testing the water content, the identical volume (20 µL) of palmatine stock solution was separately added to 1980 µL aqueous EtOH solution containing gradient water content (0%-100%, v%), with incubation for 4 min. The fluorescence intensity of the tested samples was then measured at the optimal excitation wavelength (
λ
ex
max
= 287 nm) and emission wavelength (
λ
em
max
= 538 nm) with both slit widths of 10 nm. The responsive result was calculated as F/F0, where F and F0 were the fluorescence intensity of the tested sample and the blank solution, respectively. The linearity between F/F0 value and water content was established as the working curve for the calibration of water content in EtOH. The limit of detection (LOD) was obtained according to LOD = 3δ/k, where δ represents the standard deviation of the blank signal (n = 11) and k is the slope of the linearity equation, respectively. For the recovery study, low (2.00%), medium (5.00%) and high (10.00%) volumes of water were spiked to the dried EtOH. The spiked water content in EtOH was determined according to the above procedure. The recovery was calculated by the ratio of the measured concentration (from the linearity equation) to the actual added value. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was obtained for the repetitive determination of multiple samples (n = 5) in parallel.

For selectivity experiment, the 20 µL palmatine stock solution was mixed with 1980 µL dried EtOH solution containing different individual metal ions of final concentration of 50 µM, including Ba2+, K+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Cr2+, Cd2+, Pb2+ and Hg2+. For the coexisting effect experiment, the 20 µL palmatine stock solution was mixed with 1980 µL 70% EtOH+30% H2O (v: v) solution containing different individual metal ions of final concentration of 50 µM as above. The fluorescence intensities of these solutions were then measured (λex/λem = 287 nm/538 nm), and the F/F0 values were calculated to evaluate the effect of metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of palmatine.

The similar procedure was applied for sensing water in other organic solvents, namely ACN, MeOH, Pro-OH, acetone and Diox. In the experiments, only the tested organic solvent instead of EtOH was used, and the fluorescence intensity was measured at the specific emission and excitation wavelengths corresponding to the respective solvent as listed in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitation of water content in actual samples using palmatine as the probe
ce:section.ce:para: The water content in nitroglycerin injection and disinjection alcohol was determined directly without any pretreatment as described in Section 2.3. For the recovery experiment, the fluorescence intensity of 1980 µL genuine samples spiked with purified water (2.00%, 5.00%, 10.00%, v%) incubated with 20 µL palmatine (0.01 M) was recorded (λex/λem = 287 nm/538 nm). The recovery (%) was calculated as the ratio of the detected water content to the sum of the actual water content of the sample and the spiked content.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of turn-off-(on–off) fluorescent colorimetric probe for water detection
ce:section.ce:para: The blue fluorescent N-CDs were selected to combine with palmatine to construct the turn-off-(on–off) fluorescent colorimetric sensing system. The N-CDs were synthesized according to the reported method [27]. In short, CA (0.21 g) and EDA (0.24 g) were dissolved in 5 mL deionized water to obtain a clear and transparent solution, and then transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After naturally cooling down to room temperature, the reddish brown solution was obtained, which was the N-CDs reserve solution and stored in dark. The N-CDs reserve solution was diluted 100 times with dried EtOH for use, which was diluted N-CDs solution.

The fluorescent colorimetric system was constructed by the palmatine and N-CDs (palmatine/N-CDs). Briefly, 90 µL palmatine (0.01 M) and 20 µL diluted N-CDs solution were added in 2 mL of the aqueous EtOH samples with water content of 0%-100% (v%), incubating for 4 min. The samples were then photographed under the UV dark box (λ = 365 nm), and the standard fluorescence color card was thus obtained, which could be used as the reference for visual semi-quantitation of water content in the tested samples.

Furthermore, the CIE chromaticity diagram was generated by the fluorescence spectra of the above samples. Briefly, the fluorescence spectra of the above samples excited at 365 nm were recorded and introduced into the CIE chromaticity diagram (CIE 1931) to obtain the color coordinates of (x, y).

For the smartphone-assisted on-site fluorescent colorimetric detection of water, the above samples excited at 365 nm were photographed by a smartphone; and the RGB values of the fluorescence color of the photographed samples were read through the APP (Color Acquisition). The linearity was thus established between RGB values and water content, which was used as the working calibration for smartphone-assisted analysis of actual samples.

For the actual samples, 90 µL palmatine (0.01 M) and 20 µL diluted N-CDs solution were added in 2 mL of the sample, incubating for 4 min. Then the sample was photographed under the dark box, and compared with the above established standard fluorescent color card for semi-quantitative analysis. Additionally, the RGB values of these samples were obtained by APP, and based on the above established linearity, quantitation of the water content was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Optical properties of palmatine in diversified solvents of different polarities
ce:section.ce:para:
As depicted in Fig. 1 A, palmatine possesses the isoquinoline structure, which has been well demonstrated that the absorption and fluorescence properties were significantly affected by the surrounding media, especially the polarity [28]. Herein, the optical properties of palmatine in different eight organic solvents, e.g. ACN, DMF, MeOH, EtOH, Pro-OH, acetone, Diox and DCM, and water, were studied. The polarity of studied solvents, absorption, maximum excitation (
λ
ex
max
) and emission wavelengths (
λ
em
max
), Stokes shift, quantum yield (ΦF) and fluorescence lifetime (Τ) of palmatine in these solvents are summarized in Table 1. As depicted in Fig. 1B, the UV–vis profile of palmatine in water with the obvious absorptions at 230 nm, 275 nm, 342 nm and 425 nm, was basically similar to those in other tested solvents (Table 1). Among them, the absorptions at 230 nm, 275 nm and 342 nm corresponded to the π-π* transition of aromatic benzene while 425 nm was assigned to the n-π* transition of the -C = N+- bond. However, the absorption intensity of palmatine of the same concentration in water was significantly stronger than those in other tested organic solvents. This phenomenon may be due to the strongest polarity of water among the studied solvents, and the solvents influenced the ground state of palmatine [29].

As for the fluorescence, palmatine displayed the maximum emission peak between 534 nm (in DCM) and 591 nm (in water); although there was a little variance, the maximum emission exhibited a little redshift trend with the increasing solvent polarity. The excitation independent fluorescence behavior was also observed in various solvents (Fig. 1C). As reflected in the insert of Fig. 1C, the fluorescence color of palmatine in different solvents looked quite different. Especially in ACN, the blue fluorescence was observed, which could be due to the dual emission of palmatine at 430 nm and 545 nm under the 365 nm UV excitation in this solvent, and the intensity at 430 nm was much stronger than that at 545 nm. In most other solvents, the brown-yellow to green-yellow fluorescence color appeared. In addition, the fluorescence intensity varied in different solvents, but the weakest fluorescence was observed in water (Fig. 1D). As listed in Table 1, palmatine had longer fluorescence lifetime and higher quantum yield (using quinine sulfate as reference) in nonpolar solvents than those in polar solvents. This fluorescence difference may be related to the reduction of non-radiative transition of palmatine containing isoquinoline structure in low polar solvents [30,31]. The lower the polarity of the solvents had, the higher quantum yields of palmatine possessed.

Based on the above observations, palmatine exhibited some solvatochromic characteristics. Especially in the case of the highest polar H2O, the absorption and fluorescence of palmatine was notably different. When the polarities of solvents increased, the fluorescence emission of palmatine red-shifted and the Stokes shift increased to a little degree as listed in Table 1, which was similar to the phenomenon reported in the literature [32], implying the possible intramolecular charge transfer (ICT). The ICT may be highly related to the structure of palmatine, with methoxy as the electron donating group and the -C = N+- as the electron acceptor [32,33]. For the polar solvent, like H2O, the hydrogen bond could be expected between H2O and palmatine, thus affecting the ICT process and the fluorescence was quenched. In addition, the internal charge-transfer and conformation changes may be affected by the hydrogen bond. The greater the energy lost in the nonradiative pathway, the faster the process returned to the ground state, which was also confirmed by the fluorescence lifetime in different solvents [34,35]. Hence, palmatine exhibited quite different fluorescence intensity in the studied solvents of different polarities, and possessed solvatochromic characteristics.

The optical stabilities of palmatine were further studied. The fluorescence intensity of palmatine remained basically constant after continuous UV irradiation (λ = 365 nm) for 60 min in EtOH and 70% EtOH + 30% H2O respectively (Fig. 2 A). Even being exposed to natural light for 60 days, the fluorescence intensity of palmatine in EtOH (Fig. 2B) weakened only by < 5% compared with the original, indicating the excellent photobleaching resistance of palmatine. To explore the influence of surrounding pH on the fluorescence of palmatine, the fluorescence intensity of palmatine in water with the varied pHs was monitored in Fig. 2C. At pH 2–10, the fluorescence intensity remained almost constant, but declined slightly with the increasing pH when pH > 10, possibly because ICT process of palmatine was more inhibited under alkaline conditions, resulting in the weakened fluorescence [28,36].

Based on the above analysis, palmatine had solvatochromic characteristics, and this characteristics was particularly prominent in water. Also, it had good photobleaching resistance and acceptable pH stability, implying its potential for sensing water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of water in organic solvents using palmatine as the fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: As discussed above, the fluorescence of palmatine was prominently quenched by water. Thus, palmatine was attempted as the fluorescent probe to detect water in light of the fluorescence spectrometry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of detection conditions and validation of the method
ce:section.ce:para: To better detect the water content in organic solvents, the palmatine concentration and incubation time were optimized based on the value of F’/F0’, where F’ and F0’ were the fluorescence intensity of palmatine in 70% EtOH + 30% H2O (v: v) and EtOH, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 3 A, when the concentration of palmatine varied between 20 µM and 600 µM, the corresponding responsive signal to the same water content in EtOH (i.e. 30% H2O in EtOH) varied to a small degree. As the F’/F0’ value attained slightly smallest in the presence of 100 µM palmatine, it was chosen as the best. Additionally, from Fig. 3B, the fluorescence of palmatine was rapidly quenched once it was added to the test sample, and the F’/F0’ value attained stable in less than one minute, indicating the immediate response of palmatine to water. Therefore, considering the operational flexibility, it was unnecessary to deliberately control the incubation time.

Moreover, several possible interfering metal ions [37] were considered in selectivity and interference experiments to investigate the practicability of palmatine for sensing water in organic solvents as described in Section 2.3. As depicted in Fig. S1, palmatine had no response to the studied metal ions even at a high concentration, and the presence of metal ions did not interfere with the detection of water content in organic solvents. The results demonstrated good selectivity and good anti-interference ability of palmatine.

In Fig. 3C, the fluorescence intensity weakened with the increasing water content in EtOH. The two-stage linearity between F/F0 and the water content was obtained in Fig. 3D. The linear regression equation was F/F0 = -3.524 [H2O] + 0.9532 (the correlation coefficient, R2 = 0.9885) when the water content was from 0% to 15%, and F/F0 = -0.5221[H2O] + 0.5232 (R2 = 0.9932) when 15%-100%. The LOD of the method was estimated to be 0.08% water content in EtOH.

Considering that the water miscible solvents are more likely to be contaminated by moisture which affects product performance and causes safety problems, the water contents in ACN, MeOH, acetone, Diox and Pro-OH were investigated in a similar way. As summarized in Table S1, the similar phenomena for other studied solvents to EtOH with the two-stage linearity were observed; except for Diox, only the water content between 30% and 100% was accurately determined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of water quenching effect
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the mechanism of water quenching effect on palmatine in detail, the UV absorbance and fluorescence of EtOH containing different water contents were studied. As the water content in EtOH enhanced from 0% to 100%, the fluorescence and UV spectra profile remained unchanged without marked wavelength shift; but the UV absorbance of palmatine at 240 nm weakened while the other absorption intensity remained constant (Fig. 4 A), and the fluorescence at 538 nm apparently weakened gradually (Fig. 3C). The similar phenomena for H2O quenching the fluorescence of palmatine were observed in other investigated organic solvents (data not shown). The observations suggested that no new complex was formed between H2O and palmatine, i.e. no covalent bond was formed [24]. Hydrogen bonding was then supposed to be involved for the interaction between H2O and palmatine, as this interaction could occur between H2O and methoxy groups of palmatine [24]. As a result, ICT was inhibited, and the fluorescence was quenched. The fluorescence lifetimes of palmatine were measured to be 2.21 ns, 1.62 ns, 1.57 ns, 1.56 ns in 100% EtOH, 75% EtOH + 25% H2O, 50% EtOH + 50% H2O, and 25% EtOH + 75% H2O, respectively, indicating the dynamic quenching involved in the process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quantitation of water content in real samples using palmatine as the fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: Palmatine was attempted as the fluorescent probe to determine the water content in medical disinfection alcohol and nitroglycerin injection (prepared by absolute ethanol). The water content of the disinfection alcohol (75%±5% of the labeled alcohol content) was determined to be 26.17%±0.56%, and 2.46%±0.08% for the nitroglycerin injection (100%±10% of the labeled alcohol content) with satisfactory recoveries, both of which were in line with their labelled content. Also, the results obtained were consistent with the water content measured by classical Karl Fischer method with small relative errors, 1.42% and 6.87% respectively, as compared in Table 2 . The above results demonstrate the applicability of the established palmatine probe to the genuine samples with excellent accuracy and feasibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent colorimetric detection of water content assisted by a smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: The direct application of palmatine as the fluorescent probe to measure the water content was advantageous as discussed above, but a fluorescence spectrophotometer was still needed, which greatly reduced its application universality. Hence, for the purpose of fast on-site detection, a fluorescent colorimetric system based on palmatine was constructed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the turn-off-(on–off) fluorescent colorimetric system
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, to construct the fluorescent colorimetric probe, a turn-off-(on–off) system was established based on the combination of N-CDs with bright blue fluorescence and palmatine. Once interacted with palmatine, the blue fluorescence of N-CDs was quenched by palmatine while the most of green-yellow fluorescence of palmatine remained, as depicted in Fig. 5 A, which was a turn-off fluorescence procedure. However, as depicted in Fig. 5B, with the elevated water content, palmatine may preferentially interact with water instead of N-CDs. Hence, the green-yellow fluorescence of palmatine weakened as a result of the fluorescence quenching by water; meanwhile, N-CDs were released from the palmatine/N-CDs probe, and the blue fluorescence of N-CDs was recovered, which was a turn-(on–off) fluorescence procedure. Different from the traditional turn off–on probe, palmatine/N-CDs was a novel turn-off-(on–off) fluorescent probe. When this probe worked, the blue fluorescence of N-CDs recovered gradually while the green-yellow fluorescence of palmatine was quenched further with the increasing water content; this contract change would benefit the visual detection. The FT-IR spectra disclosed that palmatine/N-CDs preserved the respective characteristic structures of palmatine and N-CDs (Fig. S2). This implied that no covalent bond formed between palmatine and N-CDs, which can also be verified by the recovered blue fluorescence of N-CDs in the presence of water as above.

As a reflection in Fig. 5C, the fluorescence color of detection system under 365 nm UV irradiation changed from green-yellow to blue corresponding to the increasing water content in EtOH. In this way, the standard color card was established as described in Section 2.5. Hence, rapid on-site semi-quantification of water by naked eyes could be realized. Comparing the fluorescence color of the two genuine samples with the standard color card, the water contents of nitroglycerin injection and disinfection alcohol were estimated to be < 10% and between 20 and 30%, respectively, as photographed in Fig. 5C, which was within the water content range as determined in Section 3.2.3.

The fluorescence spectra of samples in Fig. 5C were imported into the CIE chromaticity software to obtain multiple points with (x, y) as the coordinates. Plotting the points in Fig. 5D displayed that the solution presented a regular gradient fluorescent color transition from green-yellow (0.348, 0.524) to blue (0.185, 0.280). This CIE chromaticity agreed well with the observation in Fig. 5C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the water content in genuine samples by a smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: In the case of the smartphone-assisted colorimetric system to quantify the water content, the samples in Fig. 5C were directly taken photos by a smartphone. The RGB values of each sample were collected by the APP (Color Acquisition) as listed in Table S2, and then the correlation between B/(R + G + B) and water content was calibrated. The good linear equation was obtained (Fig. S3), B/(R + G + B) = 0.3833[H2O] + 0.3301 (R2 = 0.9936). The water contents of the above-mentioned disinfection alcohol and nitroglycerin injection were further determined via this method. The actual samples without any treatment were added with the palmatine/N-CDs and photographed (Fig. 5C). The RGB values were thus obtained and substituted into the linearity directly, and the water content of nitroglycerin was 2.70%±0.90%, and the disinfection alcohol was 26.90%±0.50%. Although the water content detected by the smartphone assisted colorimetric method had a little error with the above fluorescence method and Karl Fischer method, they were all within the labelled amount of the samples. The above results demonstrate that the smartphone-assisted colorimetric detection method had portability, good accuracy and reliability.

Table S3 compared the developed method using palmatine as the fluorescent probe in this study with the reported fluorescence methods for determination of water content. Obviously, wide linear range and acceptable sensitivity were provided by the present developed method. Meanwhile, the time required for detection of water was relatively instant, and implied the suitability for the routine analysis. In addition, the applicability of the present method was demonstrated by the genuine samples, i.e. medical disinfection alcohol and nitroglycerin injection, and the reliability was satisfactory which was verified by the traditional Karl Fischer method. More extension of the method was the visual detection and smartphone assisted on-site detection, which was attractive for fast on-site screening water/moisture content. Additionally, palmatine was a natural fluorescent molecule and could be obtained from plants, avoiding the tedious synthetic process. In a word, the palmatine probe for water detection was advantages in terms of convenience, flexibility, accuracy, wide linearity and acceptable sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, palmatine as the fluorescent probe for water detection was studied in detail. Palmatine was sensitive to water in the six selected organic solvents, including acetonitrile, methanol, ethanol, n-propanol, acetone and 1,4-dioxane. Although the detection performance differed in different solvents in terms of linearity and LOD, palmatine succeeded in determining water in these solvents, exhibiting its potential applicability to sense water in varied organic solvents. Based on the sensitive response of palmatine towards water, a novel turn-off-(on–off) fluorescent colorimetric probe was established. With the increasing water content, the fluorescence of palmatine/N-CDs gradually transited from green-yellow to blue. Fluorescent color changes could be captured by a smartphone and analyzed by APP, which made it feasible to quickly and accurately detect the water content on site without any other instrument. The developed fluorescence method and fluorescence colorimetry were successfully used to detect water contents in disinfected alcohol and nitroglycerin injection, which had reliable results in a good agreement with those by the classical Karl Fischer method. Palmatine was a potential fluorescent probe for water detection, and the developed turn-off-(on–off) fluorescent colorimetric probe was promising for on-site determination of water content with high sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
ce:section.ce:para: Ya-qian Wang: Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Qin-ying Li: Methodology. Ming Jiang: Formal analysis. Xin Yang: Formal analysis. XiaoWei Wu: Formal analysis. Xu Yu: Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Li Xu: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support of this research by the Program for HUST Academic Frontier Youth Team (No. 2019QYTD09), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21804105), and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China (Nos. 2172020kfyXJJS082) andOpenProjectFundingoftheStateKeyLaboratoryofBiocatalysisandEnzymeEngineering (SKLBEE2021014).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:section-title: Supplementary data
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107598.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:section-title: Supplementary data
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
